description: Fix all actionable known limitations documented in KNOWN_LIMITATIONS.md. This PRD addresses: busy indicator not wired up, rc-dock stale closures via React Context, workspace first-refresh race condition hardening, command capture race with workspace refresh, welcome file UX (function vs plain script), and session restore robustness. Each story is scoped to be independently testable and commitable.

userStories.title: Wire up octaveBusyTracker via wrapOctaveExecute in main.tsx
userStories.description: The busy indicator (US-S02) is fully implemented in octaveBusyTracker.ts but never activated. The `wrapOctaveExecute` Proxy approach works — it returns a new Proxy object that intercepts `octaveExecute` calls. The problem was that main.tsx tried to reassign `window.matslop` after contextBridge froze it. The fix: call `wrapOctaveExecute(window.matslop)` in main.tsx BEFORE ReactDOM.createRoot, and assign the result back to `window.matslop`. `window.matslop` itself (as a property of `window`) IS writable — only the properties OF the bridge object are frozen. The Proxy intercepts property access without mutating the frozen object. Read the comment at lines 7-14 of src/renderer/main.tsx and the wrapOctaveExecute function in src/renderer/octaveBusyTracker.ts (lines 128-152) to understand the existing code. The status bar in App.tsx already subscribes to octaveBusyTracker state (lines 154-157) — it just never gets driven because wrapOctaveExecute is never called.
acceptanceCriteria: In src/renderer/main.tsx, before ReactDOM.createRoot, call: `(window as any).matslop = wrapOctaveExecute((window as any).matslop)` (import wrapOctaveExecute from './octaveBusyTracker')
The status bar shows 'Running...' during commands that take >250ms (the debounce threshold in octaveBusyTracker)
Fast commands (<250ms) do NOT flicker the status bar
The existing octaveBusyTracker unit tests still pass
No infinite recursion or errors on HMR reload (the Proxy's idempotency guard __matslopBusyTrackerWrapped prevents double-wrapping)
Typecheck passes
All tests pass

userStories.title: Move dynamic panel state into React Context to fix rc-dock stale closures
userStories.description:
rc-dock's loadTab factory caches React elements. When App.tsx passes props that change (like helpState, cwd, octaveStatus) to panel components through the loadTab factory, those updates are silently dropped because rc-dock's PureComponent optimization prevents re-rendering cached tabs. The current workaround is `contentVersion` — a string built from all mutable state that forces a full layout rebuild when any value changes. This works but destroys component state (command window scroll position, editor undo history, etc.) on every help topic change or cwd update.

The correct fix: create a React Context (or extend the existing OctaveContext) that holds ALL dynamic state that panels need. Panel components read from context instead of props. Context updates bypass rc-dock's caching entirely because React's context propagation works through any component tree.

Specifically:
1. Create `src/renderer/AppContext.tsx` with a context that holds: engineStatus, cwd, helpState (topic + content + loading), octaveBusyState, debugState (paused, currentFile, currentLine), and any other frequently-changing values currently passed as props through loadTab.
2. Wrap the DockLayout in `<AppContext.Provider value={...}>` in App.tsx.
3. In each panel component that reads these values, replace prop access with `useAppContext()` hook calls.
4. Remove `contentVersion` from MatslopDockLayout props and the layout-rebuild logic it triggers. The layout should only rebuild on visibility/detached changes, not on data changes.
5. Keep the existing OctaveContext for engineStatus (it's already used by WorkspacePanel) or merge it into the new AppContext.

IMPORTANT: Do NOT touch the loadTab factory itself — it should continue to read from slotsRef for the initial render. The point is that the React elements it caches will internally read from context, so they stay up-to-date even when rc-dock doesn't re-call loadTab.
acceptanceCriteria: A new AppContext exists in src/renderer/AppContext.tsx with a provider and useAppContext hook
HelpBrowser reads helpState from context, not props — changing help topic updates the panel without a layout rebuild
WorkspacePanel reads engineStatus from context (can keep existing OctaveContext or merge)
The `contentVersion` prop is removed from MatslopDockLayout
Changing help topic does NOT cause CommandWindow to lose scroll position or input text
Changing cwd does NOT cause editor tabs to remount
All 12 panel types still render correctly in the dock
Drag-and-drop between panels still works
Typecheck passes
All tests pass

userStories.title: Harden workspace first-refresh: eliminate timeout race in WorkspacePanel
userStories.description:
The init-handshake race in OctaveProcessManager was fixed in US-T02 (setImmediate drain). But WorkspacePanel still has a defensive 1.5s timeout + 3-retry loop (lines 250-262 of WorkspacePanel.tsx) that was added as a workaround. Now that the root cause is fixed, simplify the refresh logic:

1. Remove the Promise.race timeout wrapper (whosWithTimeout). Just call `window.matslop.octaveExecute('whos')` directly.
2. Keep a single retry (not 3) with a longer delay (500ms) as a safety net for slow Octave startup — but document it as a safety net, not a workaround.
3. Add a 30-second AbortController-style timeout for truly hung processes (replaced the 1.5s race).
4. Make sure the watchdog timer (if it still exists elsewhere in WorkspacePanel) uses a reasonable timeout (30s, not 2s).

Also verify: after a fresh `octave:start`, the FIRST call to `whos` from WorkspacePanel resolves correctly without any timeout or retry firing. Write a test that simulates the startup sequence.
acceptanceCriteria: WorkspacePanel.refreshWorkspace no longer has a 1.5s Promise.race timeout
A single retry with 500ms delay exists as a documented safety net
A 30s overall timeout protects against truly hung processes
The first whos call after octave:start resolves within 2 seconds in the unit test
Existing workspace panel tests still pass
Typecheck passes
All tests pass

userStories.title: Serialize command capture script and workspace refresh to prevent race
userStories.description:
After every user command in CommandWindow, App.tsx runs a multi-line capture script (`__mslp_r__=pwd(); ...`) to detect figures and update CWD. This runs concurrently with WorkspacePanel's `whos` refresh. On slower systems, `whos` returns empty because the capture script holds the Octave command slot (OctaveProcessManager is single-command — the queue serializes but IPC ordering between two independent React components is not guaranteed).

The fix: make the capture script and workspace refresh sequential, not concurrent. In App.tsx's handleCommandExecuted callback:
1. First, run the capture script and await its result.
2. Then, trigger the workspace refresh (via the existing refreshTrigger counter or a direct call).
3. This guarantees the workspace `whos` runs AFTER the capture script releases the command slot.

Look at App.tsx for `handleCommandExecuted` and the capture script logic. The workspace refresh is triggered by incrementing a `refreshTrigger` counter that WorkspacePanel watches via useEffect.
acceptanceCriteria: handleCommandExecuted awaits the capture script before incrementing refreshTrigger
WorkspacePanel refresh no longer races with the capture script
The retry loop count in WorkspacePanel can be reduced to 1 (or removed if US-L03 already did this)
Running `x = 42` in CommandWindow reliably shows x in Workspace on the first refresh attempt
Typecheck passes
All tests pass

userStories.title: Change welcome file from function definition to runnable plain script
userStories.description:
On first launch, the editor opens with `untitled.m` containing a `function result = hello()` stub. Clicking Run (F5) shows a confusing 'This file only defines function(s); nothing to run' banner. New users expect Run to do something visible.

Replace the welcome content with a plain script that produces visible output when run. Something like:
```
% Welcome to MatSlop!
% Press F5 or click Run to execute this script.

disp('Hello from MatSlop!')
x = magic(3)
disp(x)
```

Find where the welcome file content is defined (search for 'hello' or 'function result' in the renderer source) and replace it.
acceptanceCriteria: The welcome file is a plain script (no `function` keyword at the top level)
Running the welcome file with F5 produces visible output in the Command Window
The welcome file demonstrates at least one MatSlop feature (e.g., matrix creation, disp output)
The 'only defines function(s)' banner does NOT appear when running the welcome file
Typecheck passes
All tests pass

userStories.title: Verify and fix session restore after rc-dock migration
userStories.description:
US-034 added session save/restore (open tabs, cursor, layout). The polish cycles migrated from Allotment to rc-dock. Session restore has not been verified since that migration.

Verify:
1. Open the app, create some state: open a file, type in command window, rearrange panels.
2. Check that session.json is being written (look at the IPC handler for session:save or layout:set).
3. Close and reopen — verify the layout, open tabs, and any cursor positions are restored.
4. If saved sessions from before rc-dock are encountered, they should be gracefully discarded (not crash).

Look at:
- App.tsx for session save/restore logic
- The main process IPC handlers for session/layout persistence
- MatslopDockLayout for savedDockLayout handling

Fix anything that doesn't round-trip correctly. Common issues:
- rc-dock layout serialization may include non-serializable React elements
- Tab ids might not survive JSON round-trip
- Floating panels might not restore
acceptanceCriteria: Opening the app, rearranging panels, closing, and reopening restores the panel layout
Open editor tabs are restored with correct file paths
rc-dock's serialized layout round-trips through JSON correctly
Old/invalid session.json files are gracefully discarded (app starts with default layout)
Typecheck passes
All tests pass

userStories.title: Verify and fix debugger against real Octave dbstop/dbcont
userStories.description:
The debugger UI (US-014 through US-023) was implemented and unit tested but never live-verified against real Octave debugging commands. The main process parses Octave's debug output to detect breakpoints, and the renderer shows call stack, watches, and breakpoint gutters.

Write an integration test that:
1. Creates a test .m file with a loop
2. Sends `dbstop in <file> at <line>` via octaveExecute
3. Runs the file via `source('<file>')`
4. Verifies the `octave:paused` event fires with the correct file and line
5. Sends `dbcont` and verifies execution completes
6. Tests `dbstep` for single-stepping
7. Tests variable inspection while paused (e.g., `disp(i)` while stopped in a loop)

Also verify the renderer-side components:
- DebugToolbar responds to paused state
- CallStackPanel shows the current frame
- WatchesPanel can evaluate expressions while paused
- Breakpoint gutter marks show in the editor

Look at:
- src/main/octaveProcess.ts: parsePausedMarker function
- src/renderer/panels/CallStackPanel.tsx
- src/renderer/panels/WatchesPanel.tsx
- src/renderer/panels/DebugToolbar.tsx
- src/renderer/EditorPanel.tsx for breakpoint gutter

Fix any issues found. Common problems:
- Octave's debug output format may differ between versions
- The paused marker regex may not match actual Octave 8.4 output
- dbstep/dbcont commands may need to be sent differently when already paused
acceptanceCriteria: Integration test demonstrates dbstop → run → pause → dbcont cycle with real Octave
parsePausedMarker correctly parses Octave 8.4's debug output format
The octave:paused IPC event fires with correct file and line number
dbstep advances one line and fires a new paused event
Variable inspection works while paused
If any debug feature doesn't work with real Octave, it is fixed
Typecheck passes
All tests pass

userStories.title: Verify and fix plotting pipeline with real Octave
userStories.description:
US-007 through US-013 implemented the plotting pipeline: Octave figure → matslop_export_fig → JSON → PlotRenderer (Plotly.js). Unit tests pass but the full pipeline has never been live-tested.

The dev environment uses extracted Octave 8.4 debs at /tmp/octave-root/octave-cli-wrap. Plotting fails there with fontconfig errors. To work around this:

1. Check if `sudo apt install octave` is available (preferred — gives a fully working Octave).
2. If not, try to fix the fontconfig issue in the extracted debs by setting FONTCONFIG_PATH or similar env vars.
3. If neither works, verify the pipeline with a mock: create a test that feeds known Plotly JSON into PlotRenderer and verifies it renders.

The pipeline works like this:
- User runs `plot(1:10)` in CommandWindow
- App.tsx's capture script runs `matslop_export_fig` after each command
- matslop_export_fig converts the current Octave figure to Plotly JSON format
- The JSON is sent to the Figure panel via IPC
- PlotRenderer uses Plotly.js to render it

Verify each step. Look at:
- src/renderer/panels/FigurePanel.tsx or PlotRenderer component
- The matslop_export_fig Octave function (should be in an m-file somewhere)
- App.tsx capture script for figure detection logic

Fix any broken steps in the pipeline.
acceptanceCriteria: The plotting pipeline from Octave figure to Plotly render is verified or fixed
PlotRenderer correctly renders valid Plotly JSON (verified by unit or integration test)
If real Octave plotting works, an integration test demonstrates plot(1:10) → figure JSON → render
If real plotting is blocked by the dev environment, a clear note is added to KNOWN_LIMITATIONS.md explaining why and what was verified
matslop_export_fig function exists and is loadable by Octave
Typecheck passes
All tests pass
